does: This paper introduces aligned training, a parameter-free reparameterization technique for sparse autoencoders that addresses critical stability and dead-feature problems. The core contribution is enforcing a geometric constraint between encoder and decoder weights such that their inner product equals one for every feature, eliminating a source of degeneracy. The method is motivated by empirical observation that SAE feature quality follows a bimodal distribution across architectures, with many features having poor alignment between encoder and decoder directions. Through this simple reparameterization, aligned training simultaneously improves reconstruction quality, eliminates dead features that are never activated, and significantly enhances stability across different training seeds, all without introducing additional hyperparameters.
connects: This work builds directly on the established line of sparse autoencoder research for neural network interpretability, which decomposes network activations into interpretable features. It responds to known limitations of standard SAEs and their variants like gated SAEs, which attempt to reduce dead features but require additional data, resampling procedures, or extra training steps. The paper connects to broader interpretability research focusing on feature quality and stability in learned representations. It also relates to work on geometric properties of neural networks and the role of alignment between encoder-decoder pairs in representation learning, extending these insights to the specific problem of SAE degeneracy and feature activation patterns.
enables: This parameter-free approach opens pathways for more reliable mechanistic interpretability of large language models and neural networks, since stable and complete feature decompositions are essential for trustworthy analysis. The method enables practitioners to apply SAEs more broadly without careful hyperparameter tuning, reducing barriers to adoption in interpretability workflows. By eliminating dead features and improving stability, aligned training makes it feasible to extract more robust causal understanding of model internals. Future work could extend this geometric alignment principle to other autoencoder variants, explore connections between alignment and feature interference, or leverage the improved feature quality for downstream tasks like circuit identification and adversarial robustness analysis.
